data.title: Comstock Resources started Buy at Clear Street as undervalued Haynesville gas play with catalysts
data.description:
Comstock Resources (NYSE:CRK) +2.2% in Thursday's trading as Clear Street initiated coverage with a Buy rating and $26 price target, saying it likes the catalysts of Western Haynesville value unlocking and deleveraging, adding the majority stakeholder is not a large overhang and the stock has adequate daily liquidity.

Clear Street analyst Tim Moore said he is taking a contrarian view on Comstock (CRK), as only 17% of current sellside analyst coverage rates the stock as a Buy, explaining his differentiated view is based on estimating the more attractive economics of the company's Western Haynesville assets, which appear undervalued by $4/share if CRK can unlock acreage for higher production and reserve value, adding that next year the company should provide enough drilling data for Western Haynesville, which might act as a reserves update to excite investors.

Moore said he favors Comstock's (CRK) first-mover advantage in the Haynesville,  with more
attractive acreage and a large productive reserve for above-average production growth and full replacement of maturity decline curves; the analyst expects the Western Haynesville to become 25% of CRK's production in the coming years from less than 10% currently, with a higher return on investment of newly drilled wells than its legacy Haynesville holdings, as the company further explores, appraises and de-risks its emerging Western Haynesville play.

data.title: Southwest targets $1B incremental EBIT from extra legroom seating in 2026 as transformation accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert Jordan highlighted continued strong execution, operational reliability, cost discipline, and the rollout of key transformational initiatives. He stated, "Southwest continues to transform at a faster pace than ever before, and I'm pleased with the results of our strategic transformation that we saw during the third quarter and the quality of the initiatives delivered. Both costs and revenue finished meaningfully ahead of expectations and the rollout and impact of our initiatives remain firmly on track."
  • Jordan noted the launch of assigned extra legroom seating in July, reporting the rollout was smooth and early results matched expectations, with a 4-point improvement in customer Net Promoter Score on aircraft with the new configuration.
  • Key product and market developments included the introduction of free WiFi for Rapid Rewards members (sponsored by T-Mobile), expanded cabins with upgraded amenities, a new partnership with Priceline, the launch of Getaways by Southwest, and a partnership with EVA Air. New market launches were announced for Knoxville, St. Maarten, Santa Rosa, and Anchorage, Alaska, all set for 2026.
  • Jordan shared, "We are entering the fourth quarter with confidence and anticipate meaningful margin expansion as the benefit from our initiatives continues to mature as we execute our transformational plan." He also signaled, "In 2026, we expect to recognize even greater benefits from our portfolio of Southwest specific initiatives, including a full year of revenue from bag fees. We expect to deliver more than $1 billion of incremental EBIT from assigned an extra legroom seating in 2026 and hit full run rate of approximately $1.5 billion in 2027."
  • CFO Tom Doxey stated, "I am pleased to once again report that we delivered strong cost performance this quarter, with CASM-X coming in at up 2.5%, beating the midpoint of our guide by 2 points, a strong beat with or without the capacity increase we saw in the quarter."

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed confidence in their full year EBIT guidance range of $600 million to $800 million. Jordan expressed, "We're confident in our ability to hit our fourth quarter and our full year guide."
  • COO Andrew Watterson projected fourth quarter RASM to be in the range of up 1% to 3%, assuming demand remains at current levels, with the benefit of initiatives and capacity increases.
  • The company expects capacity growth of approximately 6% year-over-year in the fourth quarter.
  • Jordan reiterated, "We will continue to be disciplined in our cost execution across the organization and expect that momentum to continue into 2026."

Financial Results

  • The company reported record third quarter revenue performance and expects to deliver an all-time quarterly record revenue in the fourth quarter.
  • Doxey reported "We finished the quarter with $3 billion in cash, in line with our liquidity target of $4.5 billion, including our revolver and with a gross leverage ratio of 2.1x within our target range of 1 to 2.5x."
  • An accelerated share repurchase program of $250 million was executed under the previously announced $2 billion authorization.
  • Doxey confirmed, "Our third quarter performance was ahead of our expectations for cost, revenue and net income which is another key milestone as we execute our transformational plan."

Q&A

  • Conor Cunningham, Melius Research: Asked about sequential improvement into the fourth quarter and the impact of new initiatives. Jordan explained the added capacity came from delaying retrofit timing, allowing extra revenue during the holidays. Watterson discussed government shutdown risks and demand impact.
  • Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about inflection points in load factor and basic economy buy-up trends. Watterson noted, "August, September, October had year-over-year increases in a load factor that came from the enhanced connectivity we talked about."
  • Savanthi Syth, Raymond James: Asked about the progression of RASM from Q4 to Q1. Jordan confirmed the $1 billion incremental EBIT target from extra legroom and assigned seating for 2026 is still intact.
  • Jamie Baker, JPMorgan: Probed the underlying RASM core and initiatives' contribution. Watterson argued, "Our core customers are responding back. Our brand -- our Net Promoter Scores from our customers did dip... and now have returned to where they were pre year conferences."
  • Catherine O'Brien, Goldman Sachs: Questioned shareholder return guardrails and EBIT contribution from initiatives. Doxey emphasized the importance of maintaining headroom within the leverage target.
  • Brandon Oglenski, Barclays: Asked about sustainability of the $4.3 billion in initiatives and buy-up process. Doxey affirmed, "The $4.3 billion is very much still intact."
  • Additional analyst questions focused on the learning curve for initiatives, peak initiative contribution timing, and exposure to fuel cost volatility.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on the sustainability of revenue and EBIT gains from initiatives, the impact of capacity increases, and clarity on RASM versus core business trends, often asking for greater granularity and expressing skepticism over macro assumptions.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in prepared remarks and during the Q&A, repeatedly reaffirming guidance and emphasizing strong execution, though at times hedging on guidance specifics and expressing caution around macroeconomic uncertainty.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management tone appeared more constructive, with analysts focusing on initiative ramp and the core/initiative revenue distinction, and management exhibiting increased confidence in the momentum and execution of transformational initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s strategic focus shifted from launching and ramping initial initiatives such as bag fees and basic economy (Q2) to accelerating the rollout of premium seating, new partnerships, and expanded distribution in Q3.
  • Management’s tone evolved from cautious optimism amid macro headwinds to greater confidence, highlighting record revenue and margin expansion expectations.
  • Analyst questions moved from the operational impacts of bag fees and basic economy to exploring the sustainability and run-rate of EBIT contributions from the full suite of initiatives.
  • Key metrics such as Net Promoter Score, load factor, and co-brand card growth improved sequentially, with management repeatedly citing execution and customer response.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged uncertainty around the duration and impact of the government shutdown on demand and corporate travel as a material risk.
  • Watterson discussed exposure to West Coast crack spreads for fuel, estimating it at "probably somewhere around 30 or so," with continued focus on cost discipline to offset volatility.
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the potential for RASM dilution as capacity ramps and the risk that initiative-driven benefits may be offset by macroeconomic or competitive pressures.
  • Management’s mitigation strategies include ongoing cost initiatives, efficiency programs, and maintaining flexibility in fleet and capital allocation.

Final Takeaway

Southwest Airlines management emphasized the accelerating pace and successful execution of transformational initiatives, projecting more than $1 billion in incremental EBIT from assigned and extra legroom seating in 2026 and targeting a full run rate of $1.5 billion in 2027. The company highlighted record revenue performance, reaffirmed its EBIT guidance, and pointed to positive customer response and operational resilience, while acknowledging ongoing macroeconomic risks and the need for disciplined cost execution as the transformation continues to mature.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CVB Financial outlines low single-digit loan growth outlook while expanding Southern California presence
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CVB Financial Corp. (CVBF) Q3 2025

Management View

  • David Brager, President and CEO, highlighted "net earnings of $52.6 million, or $0.38 per share, representing our 194th consecutive quarter of profitability," and underscored the company's "$0.20 per share dividend for the third quarter of 2025, representing our 144th consecutive quarter of paying a cash dividend to our shareholders." He noted an increase in net interest income and strong return metrics, with a return on average tangible common equity of 14.11% and a return on average assets of 1.35%. Brager emphasized loan growth across nearly all categories and increased loan originations, stating that "loan originations in the third quarter of 2025 were approximately 55% higher than the third quarter of 2024."

  • Brager announced the hiring of "a team of 4 bankers from City National Bank and are opening a de novo office in the Temecula, Murrieta area. They actually started yesterday."

  • E. Nicholson, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Net interest income was $115.6 million in the third quarter of 2025. This compares to $111.6 million in the second quarter of 2025 and $113.6 million in the third quarter Of 2024." Nicholson further added, "Our allowance for credit loss was $79 million at September 30, 2025, or 0.94% of gross loans."

Outlook

  • Management maintained its outlook for loan growth, with Brager indicating, "I've said kind of that low single-digit growth. And I think we can still hit that for the year. The pipelines are still strong. I feel pretty confident over the next quarter that, that should continue."

  • Brager addressed pricing competition, stating, "we are definitely willing to compete on price as long as the credit quality is where we want it to be." He also reaffirmed the company's approach: "our plan is to continue to match whatever the Fed funds decreases with decreases in money market rates over 1%."

Financial Results

  • Net earnings were $52.6 million, or $0.38 per share, compared with $50.6 million, or $0.37 per share, in Q2 2025. Pretax, preprovision income was $70 million, an increase of $1.2 million from the prior quarter.

  • Net interest income increased by $4 million over the prior quarter. Average earning assets grew by $315 million sequentially, and net interest margin improved to 3.33% from 3.31%.

  • Total deposits and customer repurchase agreements rose to $12.6 billion, up $170 million from Q2 2025.

  • Total loans at quarter-end were $8.47 billion, a $112 million increase from Q2, with growth attributed to higher line utilization in C&I and dairy and livestock lines.

  • Noninterest expense was $58.6 million, $1 million higher than the previous quarter, with a $500,000 provision for off balance sheet reserves. The efficiency ratio remained at 45.6%.

  • The sale of $65 million of low-yielding AFS securities resulted in an $8.2 million loss, offset by a $6 million legal settlement.

  • Shareholders' equity was $2.28 billion at quarter-end, with 290,000 shares repurchased during the quarter at an average price of $20.30.

Q&A

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked about deposit beta and M&A activity. Brager responded that the company reduced rates after the Fed's move and plans to "continue to match whatever the Fed funds decreases with decreases in money market rates over 1%." On M&A, Brager commented, "A lot of dialogs. Not a lot has happened yet... we're continuing conversations... there’s not anything imminent."

  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Asked about loan growth sustainability and pricing. Brager stated, "I think we can still hit that [low single-digit growth] for the year... The pipelines are still strong... The mid-single digits might be a little aggressive for the annualized."

  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: Asked about dairy and livestock line utilization and interest rate swaps impact. Brager noted new dairy relationships contributed to growth and expects normal seasonal increases. Regarding swaps, he said, "it will probably become a negative drag on our net interest income next year," but no changes to the strategy are planned.

  • Liam Coohill, Raymond James: Asked about competition on the lending and deposit side and the focus of the new banking team. Brager said, "deposit gathering has still been relatively strong," and competition comes mainly from larger regional banks. The new team will focus on operating companies and high net worth individuals.

  • Charles Driscoll, KBW: Asked about cash deployment and expense outlook. Nicholson said the company may put cash to work in the bond market if yields are attractive, and expects "low single-digit type of growth" in expenses, with continued investment in technology.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, with questions focusing on competitive dynamics, sustainability of loan growth, and strategic initiatives. No significant skepticism or negative sentiment was expressed.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, frequently expressing certainty about strategy and growth prospects. Brager used phrases such as "I feel pretty confident" and "we feel pretty good about it," indicating steady confidence across the call.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment remained similarly constructive, with analysts and management continuing to focus on disciplined growth and operational consistency.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, with management reiterating a "low single-digit growth" outlook for loans, unchanged from the previous quarter.

  • Strategic focus continued on disciplined growth, maintaining profitability, and expense control, with new initiatives including expansion into Temecula, Murrieta markets via the new banking team.

  • Key metrics saw incremental improvement: net earnings, net interest income, deposits, and loans all increased quarter-over-quarter, while the efficiency ratio remained stable.

  • Analysts' and management's tone, focus, and confidence were similar to the prior quarter, with persistent emphasis on quality growth and risk management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "rate competition for the quality of loans we focus on has continued to be intense."

  • The outlook for interest rate swaps could pose a "negative drag on our net interest income next year," according to Brager.

  • Seasonal factors impacting dairy and livestock loans and competition from larger regional banks were also noted as ongoing challenges.

  • Expense management was highlighted, with ongoing investment in technology and automation as a key area of cost growth.

Final Takeaway

CVB Financial Corp. delivered another quarter of profitability with steady improvement in key financial metrics and continued investment in long-term growth initiatives, such as expanding its banking team and opening a new office in Southern California. Management reiterated its commitment to disciplined loan growth, expense control, and navigating competitive challenges, while maintaining a positive outlook for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kaiser Aluminum raises full-year EBITDA outlook by 10% as strategic investments near completion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Keith Harvey, CEO, President & Chairman, opened by noting "another strong quarter, marking our fourth consecutive period of performance ahead of our expectations. As a result, we're once again raising our full year EBITDA outlook." He highlighted $20 million in start-up costs tied to strategic investments for aerospace and packaging, while a favorable metal pricing environment continued to support results. Harvey emphasized a 23% EBITDA margin for the quarter and over 20% year-to-date.
  • Harvey detailed progress at the Trentwood rolling mill, stating the Phase 7 plate capacity expansion project is "nearly complete" and "on time and on budget." He acknowledged the 12-week outage reduced third quarter sales by $15 million to $20 million but said the timing aligns well with customer growth expectations.
  • At the Warrick packaging rolling mill, Harvey noted the fourth coating line saw its strongest output in September and is on track to support 2026 shipments. He described positive customer feedback and reinforced the strategy of shifting output to coated products, "a segment where Warrick already holds a market-leading position."
  • Harvey reported solid demand in core end markets: "Aerospace is trending positively, though not yet fully reflected in our results. Packaging supply remains tight with strong demand expected to continue for the foreseeable future. General engineering continues to outperform the traditional 2% CAGR... Automotive rebounded meaningfully late summer after a volatile start to the year."
  • Harvey emphasized renewed cost management and a goal to restore best-in-class operating metrics as the investment cycle concludes.
  • Neal West, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Conversion revenue for the third quarter was $351 million, a decline of approximately $11 million or 3% compared to the prior year period." He attributed a 22% decrease in aerospace and high-strength conversion revenue to a 30% decline in shipments from the planned Trentwood outage and ongoing destocking. Packaging conversion revenue rose by $9 million to $138 million, with a 7% year-over-year increase. General engineering conversion revenue grew by $5 million to $81 million. Automotive conversion revenue increased 10% year-over-year to $32 million despite a 5% decrease in shipments.
  • West reported operating income of $49 million, up $36 million from the prior year, and a net income of $40 million or $2.38 per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter reached $81 million, an increase of $35 million year-over-year, despite an 8% reduction in shipments. "As of September 30, 2025, we had $577 million in total liquidity... our net debt leverage ratio improved to 3.6x from 4.3x at the end of 2024."

Outlook

  • Harvey projected continued momentum, stating, "Commercial aircraft recovery remained on pace throughout the third quarter with build rates strengthening and the supply chain normalization progressing, providing us with greater confidence of growing demand heading into 2026."
  • Aerospace shipments and conversion revenue are now expected to be down approximately 10% year-over-year due to the Trentwood outage and destocking, with recovery anticipated in Q4.
  • For packaging, conversion revenue is expected to rise 12% to 15% for the year as the mix shifts to higher-margin coated products, though shipments will decline 3% to 5% as the roll coating line ramp is finalized.
  • General engineering is projected to see full year shipments and conversion revenue up approximately 5% to 10% year-over-year.
  • Automotive full year conversion revenue is expected to increase 3% to 5% on 5% to 7% lower shipments.
  • Overall, "we're updating our full year conversion revenue guidance to be flat to up 5% year-over-year. And raising our full year EBITDA outlook by 10%, now expecting 20% to 25% year-over-year growth over our recasted 2024 EBITDA of $241 million."

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EBITDA for the third quarter was $81 million. Reported operating income was $49 million. Net income for the quarter was $40 million or $2.38 per diluted share. Adjusted net income was $31 million or $1.86 per diluted share.
  • Operating costs and inefficiencies associated with major projects totaled approximately $20 million but are not expected to continue. The company ended the quarter with $577 million in liquidity and improved its net debt leverage ratio to 3.6x. Cash flow from operations was $59 million, with $25 million in capital expenditures. Free cash flow for the year is anticipated to be $30 million to $50 million.

Q&A

  • William Peterson, JPMorgan, asked about the recovery pace for aerospace and high strength shipments. Harvey replied, "we expect that to come back very close to those levels" of the first half, with only a 5% to 10% impact from the Phase 7 Trentwood project in Q4. He expects further improvement in 2026 as build rates rise.
  • Peterson inquired about packaging contract negotiations and pricing. Harvey indicated "we're staying pretty firm with our 300 to 400 basis points increase on the EBITDA side... down to, quite frankly, one last major customer... I believe that will be finalized before the end of the year."
  • Timna Tanners, Wells Fargo, asked about tariff impacts. Harvey replied "the tariffs, we remain neutral to slightly positive from our perspective... what we've done in the marketplace is that it is a little more difficult for imports... we're seeing better demand for domestic products."
  • Tanners also asked about packaging demand amid cost inflation. Harvey said, "we may be insulated from that somewhat based on the markets that we serve. But overall, we're not seeing anyone reduce or wanting to reduce the capacities that we're contracted for."
  • On spare capacity for can sheet, Harvey clarified "we're actually fairly full right now... we've been busy shifting our capacity more to the coated side."
  • Asked about the cadence of ramp-up for new facilities in 2026, Harvey said there will be a ramp rate in the first half, but expects to see "some of these rates that we had expectations for this business" as demand improves in the second half.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on recovery timing, pricing, and ramp-up cadence, with a neutral to slightly positive tone focused on execution and risk management.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing strong execution, improved guidance, and clear improvement in financial metrics. Harvey noted "we're really looking forward to cranking this thing up beginning first of the year" and repeatedly referenced strong demand drivers.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained pragmatic with a slight increase in focus on the pace of recovery and contract negotiations. Management's confidence increased, supported by improved results and guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full year EBITDA growth was raised to 20% to 25%, up from the prior quarter's outlook of 10% to 15% growth. The conversion revenue outlook shifted to flat to up 5% versus the 5% to 10% previously projected.
  • Strategic focus tightened as major capital projects near completion. The language signaled more confidence in operational execution and end market demand.
  • Analysts' questions in both quarters centered on ramp-up timelines, contract pricing, and aerospace inventory destocking. This quarter saw more clarity on the transition to higher-margin coated products and a sharper focus on cost management.
  • Key metric changes included stronger EBITDA margin performance and improved cash flow, with net debt leverage ratio improving to 3.6x from 4.2x.
  • Management's tone is increasingly confident, while analysts remain focused on execution risks but acknowledge progress.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited variability in month-to-month demand, especially in general engineering, making it "challenging to operate with normal efficiencies."
  • Ongoing start-up costs for new facilities and operational inefficiencies were called out but are expected to taper in coming quarters.
  • Tariff-related uncertainty in the automotive sector and supply chain normalization in aerospace continue to present risks, though management views these impacts as neutral to slightly positive.
  • No new material risks or negative surprises were highlighted by analysts during the call.

Final Takeaway

Management highlighted a strong third quarter with elevated margins and improved financial flexibility, emphasizing that major strategic investments are on track to drive further gains in 2026. The updated guidance reflects greater confidence in end market demand and operational execution, with cost pressures and start-up expenses expected to subside as new capacity ramps. The company remains focused on restoring operating efficiencies and achieving its long-term EBITDA margin targets as it transitions into a new phase of growth and value creation for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Keybanc starts coverage on top travel firms Booking and Expedia
data.description:
• KeyBanc Capital Markets initiated coverage on Booking Holdings (NASDAQ:BKNG) and Expedia (NASDAQ:EXPE).
  • BKNG has an investment rating of "overweight" and a price target of $6,450, implying an upside of 23.3%; EXPE has been assigned a neutral-equivalent rating, i.e., "sector weight," and does not have a PT.
  • On Booking, the research firm likes the company's strong track record of executing against strategic initiatives; its global scale and marketing efficiency, which drives margin; "significant" capital returns to shareholders; and its niche supply and large loyalty program.
  • However, it noted that BKNG is underpenetrated in the U.S., has a maturing growth profile, and faces rising competitive pressures and regulatory scrutiny.
  • For Expedia, the research firm praised its well-known consumer brands and high-growth B2B business, among other things. Negatives for EXPE include tech and platform challenges, rising competitive pressures, and outsized exposure to the U.S., among other things.

data.title: Valley National Bancorp targets 4–6% loan growth for 2026 amid accelerated deposit expansion and operating leverage
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Valley National Bancorp (VLY) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ira Robbins announced, "Valley delivered strong results in the third quarter, reporting net income of approximately $163 million or $0.28 per diluted share. This is up from $133 million or $0.22 last quarter and represents our highest level of quarterly profitability since the end of 2022." Robbins emphasized robust core customer deposit growth, momentum in net interest income and fee income, expense control, and reduced credit costs. He added, "Our balance sheet remains extremely strong, and we have achieved many of our stated profitability goals ahead of schedule, including annualized return on average assets being above 1%."
  • Robbins introduced Gino Martocci as President of Commercial Banking and Patrick Smith as President of Consumer Banking, highlighting their roles in accelerating Valley’s strategic initiatives and customer acquisition.
  • Robbins stated, "Over the past 12 months, we've added nearly 110,000 new deposit accounts, which have contributed to nearly 10% core deposit growth." He noted indirect deposits dropped from 18% to 11% of total deposits, the lowest since Q3 2022, and the average cost of deposits fell by 56 basis points since Q3 2024.
  • Robbins cited a 15% annualized growth rate in noninterest income since 2017, attributing recent success to treasury management and tax credit advisory, which contributed $3 million of incremental revenue in Q3. Upgraded technology and expanded sales teams drove nearly $16 million of incremental deposit service charge revenue on an annualized basis since Q3 2024.
  • Robbins pointed to a "significant reduction in net charge-offs and provisions during the third quarter" and noted that cumulative commercial real estate (CRE) losses were just 57 basis points of average CRE loans over the last seven quarters.
  • CFO Travis Lan reported, "We gathered about $1 billion of core deposits during the quarter, which enabled us to pay off approximately $700 million of maturing brokered deposits." Lan noted brokered deposits now comprise 11% of total deposits. He added, "NIM improved for the sixth consecutive quarter aided by asset repricing and sequential growth in average noninterest deposits."

Outlook

  • Management guided to continued deposit cost reductions, projecting further benefits in Q4 and into 2026. Lan stated, "We are on track to achieve our above 3.1% NIM target for the fourth quarter of 2025. We expect that net interest income will grow another 3% sequentially in the fourth quarter."
  • Lan added, "We anticipate that fourth quarter fee income will be generally stable within the range of the last 2 quarters."
  • On CRE, Lan said, "We are targeting growth in CRE. I think we're looking at low single-digit growth for 2026 and beyond."
  • Lan provided a mid-single-digit loan growth expectation for 2026, "call it at a range of 4% to 6%."

Financial Results

  • Robbins reported net income of $163 million or $0.28 per diluted share for Q3, up from $133 million or $0.22 in Q2.
  • Core customer deposits grew by $1 billion, enabling the paydown of $700 million of maturing brokered deposits.
  • Brokered deposits comprised 11% of total deposits, the lowest since Q3 2022.
  • Gross loans decreased modestly due to targeted runoff in transactional CRE and C&I commodity subsegments, while CRE loans made to core clients increased. Average loans rose 0.5% during the quarter.
  • Noninterest income maintained strong momentum, with deposit service charges and wealth management performing well.
  • Adjusted noninterest expenses declined modestly, attributed to lower compensation, occupancy, and FDIC assessments, partially offset by higher third-party spend.
  • Tangible book value increased, with $12 million used to repurchase 1.3 million common shares.

Q&A

  • David Smith, Truist Securities: Asked about C&I loan decline and competitive deposit environment. Lan responded that spot deposit cost declined and expects more opportunity as they head into 2026. Martocci added, "We continue to see very strong demand both in C&I and CRE. There is ample liquidity in the marketplace."
  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group: Inquired about CRE/C&I geography. Martocci said originations are split evenly among Southeast, Northeast, and specialty businesses. Robbins highlighted Florida as a major growth driver.
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan: Sought color on nonaccrual loans. Saeger said the increase was driven by a $35 million land loan, with no loss expected, and noted 50% of nonaccrual portfolio is current on payment.
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked about white space for growth. Martocci identified upmarket C&I and business banking as key opportunities. Smith highlighted small business and retail deposit growth potential.
  • Chris McGratty, KBW: Queried on CRE book growth and lower rates. Lan said Valley is more insulated from payoff acceleration due to portfolio composition.
  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about normalized NIM. Lan projected a normalized NIM of 3.20% to 3.40% and confirmed continued hiring for upmarket C&I.
  • Jonathan Rau, Barclays: Questioned CRE refinancing and nonaccrual timeline. Saeger said office market is improving, rent-stabilized multifamily is small, and no loss is anticipated on a specific land loan.
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked about deposit growth. Lan described broad-based growth, with specialty deposits contributing but not the only driver.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on competitive pressures, loan growth, and strategic expansion, with few signs of skepticism or concern. Questions were detailed and forward-looking, reflecting confidence in management’s execution.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, frequently referencing momentum, strategic progress, and the ability to maintain positive operating leverage. Robbins said, "We have set ambitious goals for ourselves and are confident that continued execution of our strategic initiatives will deliver substantial value."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited increased confidence, with management offering more specific forward-looking targets and analysts probing for long-term growth and profitability drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q3 delivered higher net income and EPS versus Q2, with improved profitability metrics.
  • Deposit growth accelerated, with a $1 billion increase in core customer deposits in Q3 compared to $600 million in Q2.
  • Indirect deposits fell further to 11% of the deposit base from 13% last quarter.
  • Management raised guidance language for NIM and projected higher net interest income growth, while offering more explicit loan growth targets (4–6% for 2026).
  • Analyst focus shifted toward strategic hiring, upmarket expansion, and operating leverage, reflecting growing confidence in Valley’s execution.
  • Management tone was more assertive on positive operating leverage and strategic hiring compared to the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Nonaccrual loans increased due to the migration of a $35 million construction loan, but management does not anticipate a loss.
  • CRE charge-off rates in 2024 were higher than internal standards but remained below larger banks’ forecasts.
  • Rent-stabilized New York multifamily remains stagnant but is a small portfolio segment.
  • Professional fees are expected to remain elevated into the first half of 2026 due to temporary consulting costs related to efficiency initiatives.

Final Takeaway

Valley National Bancorp reported its strongest quarterly profitability since 2022, driven by robust deposit growth, lower funding costs, and disciplined expense management. New leadership in commercial and consumer banking is already strengthening customer acquisition and strategic execution. The company targets mid-single-digit loan growth for 2026, continued improvement in net interest margin, and stable asset quality, positioning Valley for sustained operating leverage and shareholder value creation in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NVIDIA using Uber driving data to further autonomous driving models
data.description:
• Shares of Uber (NYSE:UBER) are getting a lift on Thursday following an  announcement from NVIDIA (NVDA) confirming the collaboration between the two companies to further the development of autonomous driving.
  • NVIDIA (NVDA) will use Uber’s (NYSE:UBER) extensive database of real-world driving data that includes complex intersections, airports, and bad weather, to train NVIDIA’s (NVDA) AI models to improve realism and safety. By employing Uber’s (UBER) data, the collaboration will leverage NVIDIA’s (NVDA) cloud-computing platform and speed up the AV training process.
  • Thursday’s update from NVIDIA (NVDA) and Uber (UBER) builds on an earlier announcement detailing their collaboration to advance autonomous vehicle training using Uber’s vast driving data.

data.title: O'Reilly Automotive outlines 2026 target of up to 235 new stores as international expansion begins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: O'Reilly Automotive, Inc. (ORLY) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brad Beckham reported that O'Reilly delivered a 5.6% increase in comparable store sales for the third quarter, which he described as "at the high end of our expectations," emphasizing continued momentum across both professional and DIY business segments. He stated the 9% increase in operating income and 12% increase in diluted EPS reflect the team's "focus on driving profitable growth."
  • Beckham highlighted that the Professional business drove sales results with a comparable store sales increase of just over 10%, attributing outperformance primarily to Pro ticket count growth and broad-based share gains: "Our share gains have been broad-based with strong contributions from all of our market areas."
  • He noted DIY comparable store sales grew modestly, with average ticket increases offsetting some pressure on ticket counts, which he attributed to "initial short-term reaction by DIY consumers in response to rising price levels."
  • Beckham announced updated full-year comparable store sales guidance, increasing the range to 4% to 5% from the previous 3% to 4.5%. He added, "At the midpoint...an increase of approximately 2% from the midpoint of our previous guidance and a year-over-year increase of 9%."
  • Brent Kirby, President, reported third quarter gross margin of 51.9%, up 27 basis points year-over-year, and confirmed the company is "maintaining our full year gross margin guidance range of 51.2% to 51.7%." Kirby stated, "We are on track to achieve our 2025 new store opening target of 200 to 210 net new stores by year-end," and announced a 2026 target of 225 to 235 net new stores, including the beginning of expansion into Canada.
  • Kirby also revealed a reduction in full year capital expenditure guidance by $100 million to $1.1 billion to $1.2 billion, primarily due to timing on store and distribution center projects.
  • CFO Jeremy Fletcher stated, "For the third quarter, sales increased $341 million, driven by a 5.6% increase in comparable store sales and a $101 million noncomp contribution from stores opened in 2024 and 2025."
  • Fletcher detailed an updated revenue outlook for 2025 of $17.6 billion to $17.8 billion and revised full year effective tax rate guidance to 21.6%. He said, "Free cash flow for the first 9 months of 2025 was $1.2 billion versus $1.7 billion for the same period in 2024...The reduction...was primarily the result of the accelerated timing of payment for renewable energy tax credits."

Outlook

  • O'Reilly updated its full-year comparable store sales guidance to a range of 4% to 5% and increased its diluted EPS guidance to $2.90 to $3. Management expects "a mid-single-digit same SKU benefit in the fourth quarter, but have also factored into our guidance a continuation of the pressure to our DIY customers."
  • Kirby said, "We are maintaining our full year gross margin guidance range of 51.2% to 51.7% and expect to see a similar progression of gross margin rate from the third to fourth quarter as we experienced last year."
  • The company reduced its full-year capital expenditure guidance to $1.1 billion to $1.2 billion.
  • Fletcher confirmed a revised free cash flow guidance to a range of $1.5 billion to $1.8 billion.

Financial Results

  • Third quarter sales increased $341 million, driven primarily by a 5.6% increase in comparable store sales and $101 million from new stores not yet in the comp base.
  • Operating income increased by 9% and diluted EPS by 12% compared to the prior year.
  • Gross margin for the quarter was 51.9%, up from the previous year's quarter.
  • Inventory per store finished at $858,000, up 10% from last year and 7% from the end of 2024.
  • O'Reilly opened 55 net new stores in the quarter for a year-to-date total of 160 and is on track to meet or exceed its 2025 target.

Q&A

  • Gregory Melich, Evercore: Asked about the sustainability of same SKU inflation and future expectations. Fletcher responded that "most of that cost has flowed through to us" and the adjustments are "mostly behind us."
  • Melich followed up on price elasticity and DIY deferrals. Beckham explained that "there's a lot of movement," with some deferral of larger ticket jobs observed in DIY but described the situation as fluid.
  • Christopher Horvers, JPMorgan: Inquired about elasticity and store growth. Fletcher noted the cautious outlook for consumers but described unit growth in 2026 as over 4%, with Beckham adding that "expansion is officially going to start in the Canadian market in 2026."
  • Scot Ciccarelli, Truist: Asked about geographic performance and supplier risk. Beckham reported no material regional differences; Kirby addressed supplier health, specifying that First Brands is "a little bit more than 3% of our COGS" and not a significant risk due to diversified sourcing.
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Probed on DIY deferral and SG&A posture. Beckham described the situation as "still so early," with no material trade-down observed. Fletcher stated no fundamental shift in operating margin strategy, attributing SG&A trends to inflationary cost pressures.
  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Questioned the peak of inflation benefit and SG&A growth restoration. Fletcher indicated most pricing benefit is expected in Q4, but said, "We never think internally here that it's as good as it can get."
  • Bret Jordan, Jefferies: Asked about supply chain impact on inflation and inventory growth. Kirby highlighted global supplier diversification; Fletcher said inventory increase is strategic, not primarily price-driven.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised repeated concerns about sustainability of inflation benefits, price elasticity, and SG&A cost pressures, reflecting a slightly cautious tone focused on future consumer behavior and cost management.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing share gains, expansion, and operational execution, but adopted a more balanced and cautious stance in Q&A, particularly around DIY consumer pressure and inflation impacts. Frequent use of phrases such as "we believe..." and "we hope..." signaled some caution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remains confident in long-term growth but shows increased caution regarding short-term consumer reaction to rising prices, while analysts continue to probe on cost and elasticity risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Full-year comparable store sales guidance increased from a range of 3%–4.5% (Q2) to 4%–5% (Q3), and EPS guidance midpoint raised by approximately 2% from prior guidance.
  • Professional business comparable sales growth accelerated from over 7% in Q2 to just over 10% in Q3.
  • Gross margin rose from 51.4% in Q2 to 51.9% in Q3, with guidance unchanged.
  • SG&A per store growth guidance adjusted from 3%–3.5% in Q2 to at or slightly above the top end of 3.5% in Q3, reflecting continued inflationary pressures.
  • Store expansion targets increased: 2026 target of 225–235 net new stores vs. 2025's 200–210, with formal entry into Canada announced.
  • Management expressed greater caution around DIY consumer spending and inflationary impacts versus the previous quarter's more optimistic assessment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged modest but increasing pressure on DIY transaction counts due to rising price levels and possible short-term deferral of larger ticket jobs.
  • Inflationary cost pressures, especially in SG&A related to insurance, continue to be a challenge.
  • Supplier risk was addressed, with management noting a diversified supply base and no material concern from specific supplier issues such as First Brands.
  • The tariff environment remains a potential source of further acquisition cost increases, though management believes "the lion's share" of impacts have been realized.

Final Takeaway

O'Reilly Automotive delivered strong third quarter results with notable momentum in its professional business and raised full-year guidance for both comparable store sales and earnings. The company plans continued expansion, targeting up to 235 new stores in 2026 and entering the Canadian market for the first time. Management remains confident in its operational execution and ability to gain share, but is increasingly cautious about consumer responses to inflation and the evolving tariff landscape. O'Reilly's diversified supply chain and prudent cost management underpin its resilience, while ongoing investments in store growth and infrastructure support its long-term strategy.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Top 10 ETFs with the biggest exposure to Tesla in light of Q3 report
data.description:
Elon Musk's Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) after the bell on Wednesday reported a decline in quarterly profit, despite a record number of deliveries. The billionaire businessman on the earnings conference call spent a chunk of his time pleading with investors to back his compensation package that could be worth $1T.

Shares of the electric vehicle maker opened lower on Thursday and fell as much as -5.7%. However, they steadily pared their losses since and turned positive in midday trade, last +1.3%. 

The company's stock moves also affect hundreds of exchange-traded funds with exposure to it, 457 to be precise. Here are the top 10 ETFs with the largest allocation to TSLA shares:

  1. Leverage Shares 2X Short TSLA Daily ETF (NASDAQ:TSLG), 101.2% allocation. 

  2. GraniteShares 1.25x Long Tesla Daily ETF (NASDAQ:TSL), 78.32% allocation.

  3. Simplify Volt TSLA Revolution ETF (NYSEARCA:TESL), 53.34% allocation.

  1. Direxion Daily TSLA Bull 2X Shares (NASDAQ:TSLL), 32.66% allocation.

  1. T-REX 2X Long Tesla Daily Target ETF (BATS:TSLT), 31.72% allocation.

  2. Tradr 1.5X Long TSLA Weekly ETF (BATS:TSLW), 25.83% allocation. 

  3. Grayscale Bitcoin Adopters ETF (NYSEARCA:BCOR), 21.07% allocation.

  4. Global X PureCap MSCI Consumer Discretionary ETF (NYSEARCA:GXPD), 20.67% allocation.

  5. Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLY), 20.57% allocation.

  6. Roundhill TSLA WeeklyPay ETF (BATS:TSW), 19.76% allocation.

data.title: Goldman Sachs nears $1B deal to buy talent agency Excel Sports - FT
data.description:
• Goldman Sachs is nearing a deal to purchase a majority stake in sports talent agency Excel Sports, which represents sports superstars such as Tiger Woods, Derek Jeter and Caitlin Clark. 
  • Goldman's asset management unit is in late-stage discussions to purchase a controlling interest for about a $1 billion, according to a Financial Times report on Thursday, which cited two people familiar with the matter. The deal could be announced as soon as next week.
  • Excel Sports was started by sports agent Jeff Schwartz, who left talent agency IMG in 2002, to form a new firm. 
  • Goldman Sachs declined to comment to the FT. Excel didn't immediately respond to requests for comment.
